# Detector frame fixtures

Synthetic fixtures in `tests/unit/detector-fixtures.ts` are the default for focused unit tests. The committed PNG corpus is generated by `generate-detector-fixtures.mjs` and covers conditions that are awkward to represent inline: compression artifacts, motion blur, partial occlusion, unusual contrast, video-like backgrounds, controls, meters, and aspect/layout changes.

## Committed corpus provenance

All PNG files in this directory are deterministic, self-authored composites generated by `node tests/fixtures/generate-detector-fixtures.mjs`. They contain no captured video, creator artwork, faces, names, accounts, personal data, trademarks, or other third-party pixels. The procedural backgrounds, controls, meters, progress tracks, and occlusions were created solely for YapSkippr testing and may be redistributed under the repository's license. The QR module matrices encode only reserved example-domain URLs (`sponsor.example`) or the generic test URL `https://x.co/y`; neither points to source media.

The generator is the source of truth for transformations. Its fixture names and the benchmark metadata document the intended condition:

- `qr-sponsor-landscape-clean`: clean 16:9 sponsor-semantic QR.
- `qr-sponsor-small-compressed-low-contrast`: small modules, low contrast, block quantization, corner layout.
- `qr-sponsor-portrait-layout`: 9:16 bottom-left layout.
- `qr-sponsor-blur-partial-occlusion`: 3x3 blur and a small center occlusion.
- `qr-generic-url-non-promotional`: ordinary generic URL that must decode without independently creating a sponsor candidate.
- `qr-hard-negative-noisy-geometry`: high-contrast QR-like rectangles without a QR payload.
- `progress-advancing-landscape`, `progress-countdown-compressed`, and `progress-portrait-layout`: temporal positive sequences with changing fills.
- `progress-hard-negative-static-separator`, `progress-hard-negative-youtube-controls`, and `progress-hard-negative-moving-meter`: static decoration, excluded playback controls, and a moving detector-shaped meter that fusion must not surface alone.

Regenerate the assets after intentionally changing the generator, then run the benchmark and inspect the PNG diff. Do not hand-edit generated PNGs.

## Fixture requirements

Every captured fixture must have a nearby metadata entry in the consuming test that records:

- a stable fixture ID;
- the expected evidence source, such as `frame-progress-bar` or `frame-qr-code`, or an empty list for a negative case;
- a short note describing the visual condition and why the fixture exists;
- provenance: where the frame came from, when it was captured, and whether it may be redistributed;
- any crop, resize, blur, color, or compression transformations applied to it.

Keep assets compact and focused. Crop to the smallest region that still reproduces the behavior, remove audio and unrelated frames, and optimize lossless PNG files before committing them. The test loader supports PNG only and rejects other extensions so every captured fixture follows one deterministic decode path. Do not add full-resolution video captures when a small crop or generated fixture can cover the same condition.

Review every asset for privacy and copyright before committing it. Remove names, faces, account details, personal data, and unrelated creator artwork. Do not commit a frame if its source or redistribution permission is unclear; recreate the relevant geometry and contrast with the synthetic helpers instead.

## Coverage expectations

Each detector behavior should include both positive and negative coverage:

- Progress bars: advancing fills, static progress-like decoration, video controls, separators, and atypical placement or contrast.
- QR codes: clear sponsor URLs, small or low-contrast overlays, partial or compressed codes, ordinary non-promotional QR payloads, and frames without QR codes.

Name assets by behavior rather than by creator or video, for example `qr-small-low-contrast.png` or `progress-static-decoration.png`. Tests must assert the expected source labels; visual inspection alone is not an acceptance criterion.

Load a reviewed asset with `loadPngFixture` and add it to the benchmark with concrete metadata:

```ts
import { join } from 'node:path';
import { loadPngFixture } from '../fixtures/png-fixture-loader';

const frame = await loadPngFixture(
  join(process.cwd(), 'tests/fixtures/qr-small-low-contrast.png')
);
const fixture: DetectorFixture = {
  id: 'qr-small-low-contrast',
  detector: 'qr-code',
  frames: [frame],
  expectedSources: ['frame-qr-code'],
  notes: [
    'Small low-contrast sponsor URL QR.',
    'Provenance: self-created test image; redistribution permitted.',
    'Transformations: cropped to 160x90; no other edits.'
  ].join(' ')
};
```

Run the loader and focused detector suite with:

```sh
npm test -- tests/unit/png-fixture-loader.test.ts tests/unit/detector-benchmark.test.ts tests/unit/progress-bar-detector.test.ts tests/unit/qr-detector.test.ts
```

## Benchmark gates

Add representative cases to `tests/unit/detector-benchmark.test.ts` and keep their expected-source labels explicit. The benchmark reports expected detections, detections, misses, false positives, recall, and precision per detector source.

The benchmark uses minimum recall and precision gates, currently 0.90 recall and 0.95 precision for each detector source. Metrics are calculated from user-visible candidate evidence, so a raw generic QR decode or raw moving-meter progress observation is a successful low-level detection but a required candidate-level negative. Raw detection expectations remain explicit for critical coverage and false-positive fixtures. Raise gates when an improvement is proven across both positive and negative cases. Never lower a gate to accommodate a regression without documenting the failing fixture, the product tradeoff, and a follow-up issue or test that will restore the target.

## End-to-end segment corpus

`segment-detection-corpus.ts` exercises the production transcript analyzer and evidence-fusion path rather than a test-only approximation. Every case records a stable ID, video duration, expected segment boundaries, tags, and provenance. It includes:

- explicit and split-caption sponsor disclosures;
- transcript plus progress-bar corroboration;
- multiple ad reads in one video;
- QR-only open detections that remain review-only;
- negated sponsor language, ordinary call-to-action wording, end-cue-only transitions, and uncorroborated progress geometry as hard negatives.

Run both visual and segment quality gates with:

```sh
npm run test:benchmark
```

The segment gate requires at least 0.95 precision, 0.90 recall, 0.95 negative-case accuracy, 0.80 closed-boundary coverage, zero false positives per corpus hour, and at most five seconds of boundary mean absolute error. Its one-to-one temporal matching prevents duplicate predictions from inflating recall. Open detections can match by start-time tolerance, but never contribute a closed boundary.

The committed segment cases are deterministic and self-authored; they are a regression floor, not evidence of broad real-world accuracy. Add reviewed real-world-derived cases only when the transcript/evidence can be redistributed or safely paraphrased, the expected boundaries were independently checked, and the provenance is documented. Keep a separate, distinct-video holdout for model promotion so tuning cases cannot leak into reported validation quality.
